examples.title: Algorithm: Copy-on-Write Page Fault Handler
examples.description: Demonstrates the algorithm used by the OS kernel to handle COW page faults when a process tries to write to a shared read-only page
examples.code:
ALGORITHM Handle_COW_Page_Fault
INPUT: process_id, virtual_address, faulting_page
OUTPUT: None (page is mapped and made writable)

BEGIN
 # Step 1: Identify the faulting page in the process's page table
 page_entry ← GET_PAGE_TABLE_ENTRY(process_id, faulting_page)
 
 # Step 2: Verify this is a legitimate COW page
 IF page_entry.is_cow = FALSE THEN
 RETURN ERROR # Not a COW page, handle as regular page fault
 END IF
 
 # Step 3: Check if the page is still shared (reference_count > 1)
 physical_page ← page_entry.physical_frame
 
 IF physical_page.reference_count > 1 THEN
 # Step 4: Page is shared - need to make a private copy
 new_physical_page ← ALLOCATE_PHYSICAL_PAGE()
 
 # Copy the content from shared page to new page
 COPY_PAGE_CONTENT(new_physical_page, physical_page)
 
 # Update the faulting process's page table
 page_entry.physical_frame ← new_physical_page
 page_entry.is_read_only ← FALSE
 page_entry.is_cow ← FALSE
 
 # Decrement reference count of original shared page
 physical_page.reference_count ← physical_page.reference_count - 1
 
 ELSE
 # Step 5: Only this process uses the page - simply make it writable
 page_entry.is_read_only ← FALSE
 page_entry.is_cow ← FALSE
 END IF
 
 # Step 6: Invalidate TLB entry for this page (to force re-read of page table)
 INVALIDATE_TLB(virtual_address)
 
 # Step 7: Resume the faulting instruction
 RETURN SUCCESS
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows how the OS handles a page fault when a process attempts to write to a COW page. The key steps are: (1) Check if the page is marked as COW, (2) If the page is shared (reference count > 1), allocate a new physical page and copy content, (3) If only one process uses the page, simply mark it as writable. This demonstrates the lazy copying principle - actual copying only happens when a write is attempted.

examples.title: Python: Copy-on-Write Simulation
examples.description: A simulation demonstrating how COW works at the conceptual level, showing shared pages, reference counting, and lazy copying on write
examples.code:
"""
Copy-on-Write (COW) Simulation
Demonstrates the lazy copying mechanism used in OS memory management
"""

class PhysicalPage:
 """Represents a physical memory page frame"""
 def __init__(self, page_id, content):
 self.page_id = page_id
 self.content = list(content) # Make a copy of the content
 self.reference_count = 0 # Number of processes sharing this page
 
 def copy(self, new_page_id):
 """Create a new physical page with copied content"""
 return PhysicalPage(new_page_id, self.content)

class PageTableEntry:
 """Represents a single entry in a process's page table"""
 def __init__(self, physical_page, read_only=True, cow=True):
 self.physical_page = physical_page
 self.is_read_only = read_only
 self.is_cow = cow # Copy-on-Write flag

class Process:
 """Simulates a process with its own page table"""
 def __init__(self, pid):
 self.pid = pid
 self.page_table = {} # Maps virtual page number to PageTableEntry
 
 def add_page(self, vpn, physical_page):
 """Map a virtual page to a physical page"""
 self.page_table[vpn] = PageTableEntry(physical_page)

class COWMemoryManager:
 """Simulates OS memory management with COW optimization"""
 
 def __init__(self):
 self.physical_pages = []
 self.next_page_id = 0
 
 def allocate_page(self, content):
 """Allocate a new physical page with content"""
 page = PhysicalPage(self.next_page_id, content)
 self.physical_pages.append(page)
 self.next_page_id += 1
 return page
 
 def fork(self, parent, child):
 """
 COW fork: Child gets shared read-only pages
 This is the key optimization - no actual copying happens here!
 """
 print(f"\n[FORK] Creating child process {child.pid}")
 print(f" -> Sharing {len(parent.page_table)} pages with parent (no copying)")
 
 for vpn, entry in parent.page_table.items():
 # Child gets same physical page, marked as read-only and COW
 child.page_table[vpn] = PageTableEntry(
 entry.physical_page, 
 read_only=True, 
 cow=True
 )
 # Increment reference count (both processes share now)
 entry.physical_page.reference_count += 1
 
 print(f" -> All pages marked as READ-ONLY and COW")
 print(f" -> Total physical pages used: {len(self.physical_pages)}")
 
 def write(self, process, vpn, new_value, index):
 """
 Handle write operation - triggers COW if page is read-only
 Returns True if write was successful
 """
 if vpn not in process.page_table:
 print(f"[ERROR] Virtual page {vpn} not found")
 return False
 
 entry = process.page_table[vpn]
 
 if entry.is_read_only and entry.is_cow:
 # COW trigger: Page fault handling!
 print(f"\n[PAGE FAULT] Process {process.pid} writes to COW page {vpn}")
 print(f" -> Reference count: {entry.physical_page.reference_count}")
 
 if entry.physical_page.reference_count > 1:
 # Need to make a private copy
 print(f" -> Making private copy (reference_count > 1)")
 new_page = entry.physical_page.copy(self.next_page_id)
 self.next_page_id += 1
 self.physical_pages.append(new_page)
 
 # Update parent's entry to point to original (decrement ref count)
 entry.physical_page.reference_count -= 1
 
 # Update this process's entry to new page
 entry.physical_page = new_page
 else:
 print(f" -> Single user, making writable (no copy needed)")
 
 # Mark as writable, no longer COW
 entry.is_read_only = False
 entry.is_cow = False
 
 print(f" -> Write completed. Total physical pages: {len(self.physical_pages)}")
 
 # Perform the actual write
 entry.physical_page.content[index] = new_value
 return True
 
 def read(self, process, vpn, index):
 """Read from a virtual page (works on shared pages too)"""
 if vpn not in process.page_table:
 return None
 entry = process.page_table[vpn]
 return entry.physical_page.content[index]


# Demonstration
if __name__ == "__main__":
 mm = COWMemoryManager()
 
 # Parent process allocates a page with some data
 parent = Process("Parent")
 data_page = mm.allocate_page([1, 2, 3, 4, 5])
 data_page.reference_count = 1 # Parent is the sole owner initially
 parent.add_page(vpn=0, physical_page=data_page)
 
 print("=== Initial State ===")
 print(f"Parent reads: {mm.read(parent, 0, 2)}")
 print(f"Physical pages in use: 1")
 
 # Create child with COW fork
 child = Process("Child")
 mm.fork(parent, child)
 
 # Both can read (shared page)
 print(f"\n=== Both processes read (shared page) ===")
 print(f"Parent reads: {mm.read(parent, 0, 2)}")
 print(f"Child reads: {mm.read(child, 0, 2)}")
 
 # Child writes (triggers COW copy)
 print(f"\n=== Child writes (triggers COW) ===")
 mm.write(child, 0, 99, 2) # child.data[2] = 99
 
 # Verify they now have separate data
 print(f"\n=== Verification ===")
 print(f"Parent data[2]: {mm.read(parent, 0, 2)} (unchanged)")
 print(f"Child data[2]: {mm.read(child, 0, 2)} (modified)")
 print(f"Physical pages now: 2 (one copy made)")
examples.explanation: This Python simulation demonstrates the COW mechanism at a conceptual level. It shows: (1) How fork() shares pages without copying - the child gets references to the same physical pages marked as read-only, (2) How reference counting tracks how many processes share a page, (3) How the actual copy only happens when a process writes to a COW page - this is the 'lazy' part of copy-on-write. The simulation shows that after fork, only 1 physical page exists until a write triggers the copy, resulting in 2 pages.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for fork where n is number of pages (just page table setup), O(1) for read, O(1) amortized for write (copy only happens once per page)
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is number of virtual pages in page table

examples.title: C: fork() with Copy-on-Write Demonstration
examples.description: Actual C program demonstrating fork() behavior with COW, showing how parent and child share memory until written to
examples.explanation: This C program demonstrates actual COW behavior with fork(). Key points: (1) fork() returns immediately without copying the parent's memory - the child gets a virtual address space that points to the same physical pages, (2) Both parent and child initially read from the same physical memory, (3) When the child writes to shared_array[0], the kernel intercepts this, creates a private copy of the modified pages for the child, and the parent sees no change. This is why fork() is fast even for large processes - the 'copy' is deferred until absolutely necessary. Compile with: gcc -o cow_demo cow_demo.c
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for fork() with COW (just creates new page tables), O(page_size) for the actual copy when write occurs
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for page tables, O(1) additional until write triggers copy
